w:r.w:t: Remaining work divided into 4 focused sessions
Total estimated time: 11–15 hours across 4 sessions. Sessions can be run independently in any order, though S1 and S2 are recommended before S3.
NASA FIRMS fire data fetch + DB storage
Ward dropdown with pre-loaded population data
S1 DONE: Fire hotspot markers on dashboard map (orange/red CircleMarkers + legend)
S2 DONE: Multi-city live polling (dynamic city loop, 100+ cities, lat/lon from config_json)
AQI Forecast Redis/in-memory cache (1-hr TTL, Redis + dict fallback)
S3: Public advisory portal (no login needed) — START HERE NEXT SESSION
S4: CO unit labels, IDW ward AQI, real seed data

NASA FIRMS fire hotspot data is already being fetched (the FIRMS_MAP_KEY is active and working) and stored in the fire_hotspots table. The backend pipeline is complete. However, the dashboard map does not render any fire markers — the data exists in the database but is invisible to users. This is a high-impact gap because fire/stubble burning is one of the top AQI contributors in North India.
Add GET /api/v1/cities/{city_id}/fire-hotspots?hours_back=24 — query fire_hotspots table, return lat/lon/confidence/frp/detected_at
Add fetchFireHotspots() in the cities API module, wire to a React Query hook with 5-min refetch
Add a new map layer in Dashboard.tsx — render orange/red circle markers scaled by FRP (Fire Radiative Power). High confidence = red, normal = orange
On marker click show: detected time, confidence %, FRP value, source (NASA FIRMS)
Add a fire icon entry to the existing map legend so users understand what the markers mean
backend/app/api/v1/ingestion.py — add the fire hotspots GET endpoint
frontend/src/pages/Dashboard.tsx — add map layer and legend entry
Orange/red fire markers visible on the Delhi map for any hotspots in last 24h
Clicking a marker shows confidence and FRP in a tooltip
When no fires are active, no markers appear and legend still shows correctly

The CPCB data.gov.in integration now fetches real live data for any Indian city. However, the background ingestion job (poll_all_stations_job) has DELHI_CITY_ID hardcoded — so if an admin onboards Indore, Bengaluru, or any other city, zero real readings will ever be pulled for it. The system will only serve mock data for non-Delhi cities. This is a correctness gap that undermines the multi-city value proposition.
Dynamic city list in ingestion job
Replace hardcoded DELHI_CITY_ID in poll_all_stations_job with a DB query that fetches all active cities, then loops and polls each one
Extend _city_to_state() in caaqms.py to cover all cities in INDIA_STATES (currently covers ~20; needs all 36+ cities)
fetch_weather_for_delhi() is hardcoded to Delhi lat/lon (28.6, 77.2). Store lat/lon in city.config_json and read from there; fall back to Delhi coords if absent
fire_hotspots.py uses DELHI_BBOX hardcoded. Derive bbox from city centroid (station average) or config_json; default to ±0.5 deg square
On app startup (lifespan), trigger one poll cycle for all cities so the dashboard is never empty on first load
Adding Indore via Admin panel and triggering a poll returns real CPCB PM2.5 values (31–59 µg/m³ as verified today)
Weather readings appear for any city with lat/lon in config_json
Fire hotspot polling uses correct bounding box for each city

All advisories are generated and stored but locked behind admin login. A citizen who wants to know today's air quality and health guidance has no way to access VayuShield without credentials. This is the biggest value delivery gap — the platform's primary promise is to protect citizens, but citizens cannot see it. A simple public-facing page shareable as a URL would demonstrate real-world impact in the hackathon demo.
Add /city/:cityId/public route in React Router. Exempt this route from the auth guard in App.tsx
Add GET /api/v1/cities/{city_id}/public/summary — returns current AQI, AQI level, dominant source, latest advisory title+body in en and hi. No auth required.
Large AQI number card with color coding (Good=green, Moderate=yellow, Poor=orange, Severe=red), city name, last updated time
Show the AI-generated advisory title and body text. Language toggle button (EN / हिं) to switch between English and Hindi
Based on AQI level, show 3 action cards: Stay Indoors / Wear N95 / Avoid Outdoor Exercise — with icons, conditionally rendered
Show PM2.5, PM10, NO2 values from latest station readings with unit labels (µg/m³)
Copy-to-clipboard button for the public URL so users can share the link on WhatsApp/social media
Dropdown to switch between available cities on the public page without logging in
frontend/src/App.tsx — add /city/:cityId/public route outside auth guard
backend/app/api/v1/public.py — new router, no auth dependency
Page shows current AQI number, color, advisory text in English
Language toggle switches to Hindi advisory
URL is shareable — opening in incognito works without redirecting to login
Looks presentable enough to screenshot for the hackathon submission

Several data accuracy issues exist that would be noticed during a careful demo review or by a technical judge. These do not break functionality but reduce trust in the platform. This session addresses unit inconsistencies, historical data realism, and display gaps that make the difference between a prototype and a credible product.
CPCB API returns CO in µg/m³ but the UI shows no unit label, making 62 µg/m³ look alarming. Add unit labels to all pollutant displays: PM2.5 µg/m³, PM10 µg/m³, NO2 µg/m³, CO µg/m³, O3 µg/m³.
Historical seed using real CPCB snapshots

The 168 mock readings per station use a sine-wave generator. Replace with a loop that calls the real CPCB API at seed time and fills only missing hours with mock. This makes charts look real for Delhi.

Currently each ward is assigned the AQI of its nearest single station. With 2 stations, half the wards get one value and half get another. Implement inverse-distance weighting across all active stations for smoother, more realistic ward maps.
Some CPCB stations return NA for certain pollutants (e.g. Maguda Nagar has no PM2.5). Currently these show as null or 0 in the UI. Display a dash (—) with a tooltip explaining the sensor is offline.

The 72-hour forecast diurnal model works correctly but when seeded data is mock, the chart looks flat/repetitive. After fixing the seed (task 2 above), re-trigger a forecast run so the chart reflects realistic patterns.
Generalise the map center to use the selected city's centroid instead of hardcoded Delhi (28.6, 77.2). Read from station average coordinates.
frontend/src/pages/Dashboard.tsx — map center from city centroid
frontend/src/pages/WardDetail.tsx — IDW interpolation display, unit labels
backend/app/db/seed.py — real CPCB data fetch at seed time
All pollutant values on dashboard show correct units (µg/m³)
NA sensors show — instead of 0 or null in station table
Delhi ward map shows gradient variation across wards, not just 2 discrete values
Forecast chart for Delhi shows realistic diurnal pattern (higher morning/evening, lower midday)
Selecting Indore in Admin panel and viewing dashboard centers map on Indore, not Delhi
